item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is both painful and beautiful. I'm not ashamed to say it brought tears to my eyes at multiple points.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is absolutely amazing. I shed a tear at the end. The image of the skip petting wild animals, promoting mating behavior and increasing survival rates of wild life close to it really impressed me, it gave that section of the article a very good and well described sense of hopefulness. Good job!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was beautiful and horrible; bleak and hopeful. Really good read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's a coincidence. Sort of.

I'm working from the same base as Asimov; Gaia is the Ancient Greek goddess of the Earth, and the basis for Mother Earth and Mother Nature ideas.

The concept itself came from 'what we killed Mother Nature, and how would the Foundation react?'

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great article! Is the title+concept a reference to the Foundation series by Asimov, or is that a coincidence?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was great. "Mother Nature died, so we brought her back. She was angry, so we killed her again. Then we made our own." Classic Foundation.

I also appreciate that, while the thought occurred to me early on that you were just dropping telepathy as a thing people can do into this article and it didn't feel necessary, you made it necessary. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You had some great characterization in there for the agent and the AI. I also loved the message and its delivery. The narrative arc was also well-executed; you had the standard introduction/exposition, conflict, darkest hour, climax, resolution thing going very well. I know some people disagreed, but I enjoyed the magitechnobabble; I think it went on just long enough and had just enough detail for me to understand that this was an expert in the field discussing their area of expertise with a non-layman, but not so long that my eyes glazed over.

You might think there's a "but" or a "the one thing I didn't like" coming, but much like this skip, this ends on a happy note. Well done!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The lack of a twist at the end is a surprise, and a very welcome one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe Archon is unnecessary, as others noted. But here, it works. This is a pretty good scp, which also demonstrates why neutralizing is a last resort only.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The pseudo-science is so much filler that I had to skim hardcore. Which is such a shame, cause at first I was cringe at the idea "all life makes mother earth", and we killed it. Then you went and had the Foundation remake it, and then it backfires, and then you get agent Yen.

There's enough character development and meat to the skip to make me upvote.

But as I said, I had to skim past the parts where you painstakingly detail how magic works. I've upvoted worse skips than this simply cause the rule is if I can read to the end they deserve to live. A sad downvote from me, but I'm trying to be as fair as possible with this binary voting system.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
An optimistic SCP?

On your Wiki?

It's more likely than you think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An optimistic SCP? About the future of Nature as a whole and humans? In my site? Man, I don't know if I am ready about this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good SCP, but I sort of feel like this was copied from the planet Gaia from Foundation's Edge. Or you were just inspired by it.
The addition of an AI and the new Claire-Gaia were quite interesting, but I think it would go better as a tale. But that's just my opinion.
Good job. I quite liked this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Apotheosis of a Foundation agent is a little terrifying, but they've still got the procedure for Operation Scythe in their back pocket for emergencies.

Thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a lovely balance of comforting and creepy. The narrative/conversation structure is subtle enough that the ending is a surprise, but at the same time you leave enough breadcrumbs for it to make sense.

I am a little terrified at the prospect of a Foundation agent who effectively achieves godhood, but I'm pretty sure that's the point. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is such a good SCP. It's optimistic, wide scale and very easy to understand. I loved the tidbit about Gaia dying during mass extinctions including the current Anthropocene and how the Foundation explicitly funds those who are helping the Earth. The latter makes me very happy. It's always funny that the Foundation made sure to include the concerns about its their goals and normalcy even when it comes to the entity that represents all life on Earth.

I hope there is more involvement with this SCP in tales and whatnot in the future. You did a great job and I give it a +1. It's a new favorite for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The ending made me tear up a little… Such a good reading.
But i can't help but smirk and shake my head — of course the foundation had to butt in their interest on the same level as the entire biosphere well beibg. Oh, foundation, never change :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I mostly lurk around the site and this may even be my first comment, but I’m so glad I randomly clicked on this SCP. The structure, tone and narrative combine in a really nice way for me and this is right up there with some of my favourite SCPs of all time. A well deserved +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconding what Feros said, nice SCP. I personally like the use of Archon; it feels appropriate here. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i really liked this one, the concept was interesting, and its one of the few SCPs that has a happy ending. i think we need more of that. anomalies dont always have to cause the end of the world or brutally kill everyone in a 100 meter radius to be cool or interesting. more often than not, the skips that arent really dangerous tend to be the most interesting. its also pleasing to see the Foundation being competent and actually managing to contain the anomaly and protect the rest of the world. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I absolutely enjoyed and loved this SCP, as well as her awesome AI Helper. I personally like the idea and concept of a Mother Earth that can feel the same emotions and pain as normal humans do, and how our acts of mass pollution and environmental destruction directly impacts her health.
+1 from me. This SCP deserved it :3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is quite possibly my new favourite Series 5 article, easy +1
absolutely loved it

Don't listen to them Archon haters :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it. (Though I do agree with the above about the Archon class not really being necessary.) +1.

A couple proofreading things:

  • You have a stray "the" in the second document — it says "the Costa Rica".
  • It's spelled "utilitarian", not "utalitarian". (Unless you're using some terminology I'm unfamiliar with.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ah, but her absence doesn't cause an anomaly.

Her absence leaves us at the default state, and the default state of life is failure (especially once our environmental destruction is considered). This SCP thus proposes that while life itself is not necessarily an anomaly, it's incredible success and propagation is.

While logically your argument is completely correct, and I would normally concede and edit appropriately, I've made these decisions for deliberate thematic reasons.

If it's any consolation, I'll point out that I was considering using the Apollyon class for 4043-B for quite a while.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But the new Claire-Gaia doesn’t exist in isolation - her existence is inexplicably tied to life on Earth as we know it. She is an anomaly needed to contain the anomaly that her nonexistence causes mass extinctions. Hell, she was created by the Foundation. So she has to be Thaumiel.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While the first usage could be Keter, that would come at the cost of losing the impact of the third object class (Keter-Neutralised-Keter) doesn't have the same punch. Also, on a meta level, if Archon is something that we explicitly shouldn't restrict, lest we face dire consequences, then our biosphere and natural habitats are real life Archon entities. (If I'm allowed to be a preachy environmentalist.)

The final usage however explicitly can't be Thaumiel. The Thaumiel class, by definition, is used for anomalies that are used to answer other anomalies. And Gaia mostly exists in isolation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Archon class remains hilariously unnecessary (the first usage of it should be Keter, the second Thaumiel), and some of the psychic gobbledegook could have been trimmed down, but I very much enjoyed the framing device and the plot.

item.title: Author Note
item.content:encoded: Thanks go out to Gummy_Dragon for the review and feedback.
